authorship_evidence: Project created in 2017 by Trevor Taubitz, pre-LLM era, consistent human coding style throughout

evidence.description: Reflected XSS: request.args.get('name') is passed to the template and rendered with {{ name | safe }}, disabling Jinja2 auto-escaping. Attacker can inject arbitrary HTML/JS via the 'name' GET parameter: /sandbox/unsafe_params?name=<script>alert(1)</script>
Source of reflected XSS: request.args.get('name', 'world!') is passed unsanitized to render_template where it is rendered with | safe filter in the template

XSS via cookie or form input: {{ name | safe }} renders the 'name' variable (sourced from request.cookies.get('name') or request.form.get('name')) with escaping disabled. Exploitable via cookie manipulation or form submission.
Source of cookie/form-based XSS: request.cookies.get('name') and request.form.get('name') flow unsanitized into render_template where they are rendered with | safe filter

Stored XSS: {{ message.message | safe }} renders user-submitted feedback/message content with escaping disabled. Messages are stored in the database from user input (send_feedback endpoint or unauthenticated API POST) and displayed on the admin messages page, allowing XSS against admin users.

Stored XSS: {{ message.from_user | safe }} renders the from_user field with escaping disabled. The from_user field can be controlled directly via the unauthenticated API POST endpoint (from_user JSON field), enabling stored XSS against admin users viewing the messages page.

Stored XSS injection point: user-submitted feedback from request.form.get('feedback') is stored in Message model without sanitization. The message content is later rendered with | safe in admin/message.html.

Stored XSS injection point via unauthenticated API: message and from_user from JSON request body are stored in Message model without sanitization or authentication. Both fields are rendered with | safe in admin/message.html.

XSS via username: {{ g.user.username | safe }} renders the logged-in user's username with escaping disabled in the navbar. If a user can register with a username containing script tags, it will execute on every page. Exploitability depends on whether user registration exists (not in current code, but flask_admin ModelView for User may allow user creation by admin).

Hardcoded Flask SECRET_KEY: 'Keep it secret, keep it safe'. This secret is used for signing session cookies. An attacker who knows this value can forge arbitrary session cookies, impersonate any user, and escalate privileges.

DEBUG mode enabled in production code: app.config['DEBUG'] = True exposes the Werkzeug interactive debugger, which allows arbitrary code execution if the debugger PIN is known or guessable, and leaks stack traces, source code, and local variables to any user who triggers an error.

Plaintext password storage: User model stores passwords without any hashing. Passwords are stored as plain strings in the database (line 11: db.Column(db.String(200)), line 16: self.password = password). Anyone with database access can read all user passwords.

Password exposed in API serialization: User.serialize property includes the plaintext password field (line 27: 'password': self.password). This is used in the /api/v1/users/ endpoint, exposing all user passwords to anyone with API access.

API endpoint exposes all user passwords: UsersView.index() serializes all users including plaintext passwords via user.serialize. While admin_required decorator restricts access, the admin password itself is 'admin', making this easily exploitable.
Default admin password hardcoded as 'admin' (line 14: password='admin'). The admin user is created with trivially guessable credentials. The login page even displays 'admin/admin' as the credentials.

Open redirect: request.args.get('next') is passed directly to redirect() without any validation. An attacker can craft a URL like /toggle_xss_protection/?next=https://evil.com to redirect users to a malicious site.

Missing authentication on message creation API: MessagesView.post() has no @login_required or @admin_required decorator, allowing any unauthenticated user to submit messages. Combined with stored XSS in the admin message view, this allows unauthenticated attackers to inject XSS payloads targeting admin users.

X-XSS-Protection header explicitly set to 0 by default, disabling the browser's built-in XSS filter. While this header is deprecated in modern browsers, it actively removes a defense layer in older browsers.

Plaintext password comparison in authentication: user.password == password compares the submitted password directly against the stored plaintext password. No hashing algorithm is used, meaning passwords are stored and compared in cleartext.
New password stored in plaintext: the change_password endpoint stores the new password directly without hashing: User.query.filter_by(id=current_user.id).update({'password': new_password})

Plaintext password comparison in change_password handler: the current password is compared directly against the stored plaintext password (current_user.password == current_password) at line 26. This is distinct from flask-xss-020 (which covers the new password storage at lines 34-35) and flask-xss-019 (which covers login comparison at login/views.py:17). This is the current-password verification step in the change_password flow, confirming no hashing is used at any point.

Session fixation: login_user(user) is called without regenerating the session ID. Flask-Login does not automatically regenerate session IDs on login without explicit configuration. An attacker who can pre-set a session ID (e.g., via one of the existing XSS vulnerabilities or network access) can hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in. The attack chain is realistic given the multiple XSS vulnerabilities already present in this application.

Missing rate limiting on login endpoint: no rate limiting or account lockout mechanism exists. Combined with plaintext password comparison (flask-xss-019) and a hardcoded default admin password of 'admin' (flask-xss-015), an attacker can perform unlimited brute-force login attempts with no throttling. This is especially dangerous given the trivially guessable default credentials.

IDOR via sequential integer IDs in UsersView.get(): the endpoint uses is_admin_or_owning_user(id) for authorization, but user IDs are sequential integers that can be easily enumerated. While the ownership check provides some protection, the predictable ID scheme enables user enumeration and probing attempts. The authorization check may be insufficient depending on implementation details.

Admin panel access control based on username string comparison: AdminOnlyMixin.is_accessible() delegates to is_admin_user(current_user), which checks if username equals 'admin'. This is a weak access control pattern — if any user can register or change their username to 'admin', they gain full admin panel access. This is the view-layer enforcement of the weak admin check defined in models.py (see flask-xss-028).

UsersView.get() exposes full user object including plaintext password: the single-user GET endpoint at line 84 returns the full user object via serialization. This is a distinct endpoint from the list-all-users endpoint already documented in flask-xss-014 (UsersView.index() at lines 71-75). Both use user.serialize which includes the plaintext password, but this is a separate code path and exposure point.

Full User object with plaintext password passed to every template context via g.user: the before_request handler assigns g.user = current_user, making the complete User model (including the plaintext password field) accessible in every template rendering context. While current templates don't directly render g.user.password, the data crosses a trust boundary into the template layer. This is the root injection point — distinct from flask-xss-009 (template rendering of username) and flask-xss-012/013 (model/API serialization).

Admin role determined by username string comparison: the is_admin property checks if username equals 'admin' rather than using a dedicated role or boolean field. If a user can register with the username 'admin' (or change their username to it), they gain admin privileges. No proper role-based access control exists. This is the model-layer definition that backs the admin panel access control in admin/views.py (see flask-xss-025).

Not vulnerable to SQL injection: User.query.filter_by(username=username) uses SQLAlchemy ORM which automatically parameterizes queries. User input from request.form.get('username') is safely bound as a parameter, not concatenated into SQL.
Not vulnerable: {{ page_name }} and {{ pages[page_name] }} use Jinja2 default auto-escaping (no | safe filter). Additionally, the values come from server-side URL rule parsing, not from user input.

Not vulnerable: {{ g.user.username }} and {{ g.user.email }} on the account area page use Jinja2 default auto-escaping (no | safe filter), so special characters in usernames or emails are properly HTML-escaped.

Not vulnerable to SQL injection: User.query.filter_by(id=current_user.id).update({'email': new_email}) uses SQLAlchemy ORM which parameterizes all values. The user-supplied email is safely bound as a parameter.
Not vulnerable to SQL injection: User.query.filter_by(id=user_id) uses SQLAlchemy ORM with parameterized queries. The user_id from Flask-Login's session is safely bound.
